Q5.text: You care about someone. How do they know?

answers.A: You tell them directly
answers.B: You show up consistently without making a big deal of it
answers.C: You do small things and hope they notice
answers.D: You tease them -- that's just how you are

Q6.text: A friend cancels plans last minute.

answers.A: Mild disappointment but you understand
answers.B: More bothered than you let on
answers.C: Honestly, kind of relieved
answers.D: You're already texting to reschedule

Q8.text: What does 'close' actually mean to you in a friendship?

answers.A: Picking up exactly where you left off after months apart
answers.B: Knowing what's going on with each other on a regular basis
answers.C: Being in the same space a lot, doing your own things
answers.D: Rare but deep conversations

Q9.text: You're genuinely good at something. How do you carry that?

answers.A: You know it, you just don't broadcast it
answers.B: You're still not fully convinced -- might just be luck
answers.C: You've worked for it and you own it
answers.D: You'd rather the results speak than claim it yourself

Q13.text: Things are going wrong and there's no clear fix.

answers.A: Get methodical -- break it down, find the next step
answers.B: Lean on someone who can help you think through it
answers.C: Absorb it alone until you have something actionable
answers.D: Find the one thing you can actually control and go there

Q14.text: Someone challenges you on something you're confident about.

answers.A: Hold your ground until they make a compelling case
answers.B: Hear them out genuinely -- maybe they see something you don't
answers.C: Agree outwardly even if you're not convinced
answers.D: Push back immediately

Q16.text: The most draining kind of conflict is...

answers.A: When the other person won't engage honestly
answers.B: When you're in the wrong and you know it
answers.C: When there's no resolution possible
answers.D: When it's with someone you actually care about

new_questions.text: Someone you respect gives you feedback in front of a group. It stings, but they're not wrong.

answers.A: You take it, say thanks, and move on -- no point making it a thing
answers.B: You push back a little in the moment, even if you know they're right
answers.C: You go quiet, absorb it, and deal with the embarrassment privately
answers.D: You crack a joke to break the tension, then figure it out later

new_questions.text: There's someone on your team who keeps falling behind. What actually goes through your head?

answers.A: Frustration -- it slows everyone down and someone has to pick up the slack
answers.B: Concern -- you want to know what's going on and whether you can help
answers.C: Mild impatience, but it's not your problem unless it affects you directly
answers.D: It motivates you -- seeing the gap makes you want to pull them up with you

new_questions.text: What are you actually chasing when you push yourself hard?

answers.A: The recognition -- you want people to see what you can do
answers.B: The feeling -- there's a rush in going all-out that has nothing to do with the result
answers.C: Proving it to yourself -- what anyone else thinks is secondary
answers.D: The group -- you're working for something bigger than your own performance

new_questions.text: A new person joins your team and immediately starts performing well. Your first instinct?

answers.A: Excitement -- fresh energy, and now there's someone worth competing against
answers.B: Curiosity -- you want to get to know them before you decide what to think
answers.C: Watchfulness -- you're paying attention to see if they're as good as they look
answers.D: Warmth -- you go out of your way to make them feel like they belong

new_questions.text: You've been going hard for a while and you're genuinely worn down. What do you actually do?

answers.A: Keep the energy up -- you'd rather burn out than let people see you flagging
answers.B: Tell someone you trust you need a minute, then recharge and get back to it
answers.C: Take the break without making an announcement -- you don't owe anyone an explanation
answers.D: Use it as a reason to drag someone into downtime with you

new_questions.text: You're clearly winning at something and the other person is not having a good time. Do you ease up?

answers.A: No -- easing up is its own kind of disrespect
answers.B: You read the room and adjust if they actually want you to
answers.C: You keep going but try to make it more fun for them in the process
answers.D: You'd rather they enjoy themselves than you run up the score

new_questions.text: Something went badly and it's at least partly your fault. Who do you tell?

answers.A: You own it out loud, right away, to whoever needs to know
answers.B: You tell the people directly affected -- not everyone else's business
answers.C: You fix it first, then bring it up -- context matters more than the confession
answers.D: You sit with it for a while before you say anything

new_questions.text: What does a genuinely good day actually look like for you?

answers.A: You did something you didn't think you could do
answers.B: You moved fast, got loud, and felt completely alive the whole time
answers.C: The people you care about had a good time, and you were part of it
answers.D: Someone noticed what you pulled off and said so

new_questions.text: Sometimes the most honest version of what you're feeling comes out...

answers.A: Straight -- you don't really have a filter for it
answers.B: As a joke you're only half-kidding about
answers.C: Through a character or voice you've invented that says it for you
answers.D: After the fact, in something you do rather than anything you say

new_questions.text: You do something well and someone tells you directly. What happens?

answers.A: Something warm flickers, but you move past it before it fully lands
answers.B: You take it in. You file it. You've earned it.
answers.C: You say thank you and then quietly wonder if they really meant it
answers.D: You point to what still needs work -- that's where your head already is

new_questions.text: When things feel like too much, what actually steadies you?

answers.A: Being near a specific person -- proximity alone does something
answers.B: Finishing something small -- anything you can check off and close
answers.C: Sitting with it alone until you've processed enough to move
answers.D: Focusing on someone else's needs -- it gets you out of your own head

new_questions.text: There's something that bothers you quietly. You haven't mentioned it. What is it?

answers.A: A fear that your results are flukes -- you haven't actually earned where you are
answers.B: A worry that someone important is going to stop needing you
answers.C: A sense you're invisible unless you're actively being useful
answers.D: A feeling you're falling short of a standard only you can see

new_questions.text: You're ahead of schedule. You showed up prepared and there's nothing left to do yet. What actually happens?

answers.A: You check your prep again -- you probably missed something
answers.B: You find something to do for someone else
answers.C: You feel briefly calm, then locate the next thing to worry about
answers.D: You sit with it. You let yourself have the moment.

new_questions.text: Pressure is rising and you know it. What happens to how much space you take up?

answers.A: You get louder and faster -- moving before you've thought it through
answers.B: You get smaller on the outside while working harder underneath
answers.C: You redirect outward -- find whoever needs you and go there
answers.D: You get quieter and more precise, doing more than before

new_questions.text: Someone close to you is getting the kind of attention you needed. What do you actually do?

answers.A: Work harder -- if they're getting it, you haven't done enough yet
answers.B: Go a little quiet, then wonder why it's hitting you as hard as it is
answers.C: Tell yourself their win counts as yours and mostly believe it
answers.D: Find a way to be more visible -- show up more, offer more

new_questions.text: You did something embarrassing in front of someone you wanted to impress. What's the next thing you do?

answers.A: Laugh it off out loud before anyone else can react
answers.B: Correct course immediately -- give them something better to remember
answers.C: Apologize more than necessary, which makes it worse
answers.D: Pretend it didn't happen and carry on at full volume

new_questions.text: Someone in your life keeps getting more recognition than you, and you both know it. How does that sit with you?

answers.A: Fine -- you're focused on your own lane anyway
answers.B: Quietly motivating. You're not going to say anything, but you noticed
answers.C: It bothers you more than you'll admit, and it comes out sideways
answers.D: You just say it: you want what they have, and you're going to get it

new_questions.text: You've decided you care about someone. What does that look like from the outside?

answers.A: You show up whenever they need something, no announcement required
answers.B: You find reasons to be near them and make it their idea
answers.C: You tell them, plainly, and then you prove it through actions
answers.D: You get louder and funnier around them -- attention is how you reach people

new_questions.text: You messed something up and you can't stop thinking about it. What eventually gets you out of it?

answers.A: Someone tells you it's okay -- and you actually believe them this time
answers.B: You make a plan to do it better next time and start immediately
answers.C: Time. You need to sit with it before you can move
answers.D: You find a way to laugh at yourself and that usually breaks it open

new_questions.text: You're doing well at something and someone's watching. How does that change your performance?

answers.A: You go harder -- the audience is the fuel
answers.B: You get more careful, worried you'll ruin it now that it matters
answers.C: It doesn't change much; you were already giving everything
answers.D: You're aware of them but you keep your focus where it belongs

new_questions.text: Something important fell through and it wasn't your fault. What do you want from the people around you?

answers.A: Acknowledgment that you handled it well despite circumstances
answers.B: Practical help pivoting to whatever comes next
answers.C: Someone to just sit with you for a minute without trying to fix it
answers.D: To be left alone until you've processed it yourself

new_questions.text: You've been working hard at something for a long time with no recognition. What do you do?

answers.A: Work louder -- make the effort impossible to miss
answers.B: Find one person whose opinion matters and ask them directly
answers.C: Decide you don't need recognition and double down on the work itself
answers.D: Accept that you might be doing this for yourself and adjust your expectations

new_questions.text: Someone pushes back on something you said and they're partially right. How do you respond?

answers.A: Acknowledge what's right, hold what you still believe, and say both
answers.B: Get defensive first, then come around later when you've thought about it
answers.C: Cave immediately -- conflict feels worse than being wrong
answers.D: Counter-argue the whole thing even though you already know they have a point

new_questions.text: The mission just ended. You have about two hours before anything else is scheduled. What do you actually do with that time?

answers.A: Start reviewing what just happened and note what to do differently next time
answers.B: Find something you personally enjoy -- a hobby, a show, a drink -- and actually unplug
answers.C: Check in on people. Make sure everyone landed okay and nobody needs anything
answers.D: Migrate straight into whatever's next. Idle time feels wrong.

new_questions.text: Someone on your team makes a small but avoidable mistake. You noticed it. What happens?

answers.A: You pull them aside and walk through it. You want them to understand, not just fix it.
answers.B: You let it go this time unless it becomes a pattern -- everyone has off moments
answers.C: You log it internally but don't say anything. You'll adjust how you work with them going forward.
answers.D: You mention it lightly in the moment, then move on. It doesn't need to be a whole conversation.

new_questions.text: You want to do something nice for someone you care about. Which approach feels most like you?

answers.A: Think through what they actually need right now and quietly handle it before they have to ask
answers.B: Make something -- something personal and specific to them, even if it takes time
answers.C: Tell them directly. You'd rather say it out loud than leave it to inference.
answers.D: Build in a shared experience. Do something together rather than give them a thing.

new_questions.text: You're in a meeting where someone is clearly wrong, but they're senior to you. What do you do?

answers.A: Speak up. Rank doesn't make something correct.
answers.B: Flag it privately afterward. Public correction usually backfires.
answers.C: Wait and see -- sometimes the full picture comes out on its own
answers.D: Document your concern and bring it up through the proper channel later

new_questions.text: Your workspace or living space. What does it actually look like on a normal day?

answers.A: Organized by a system that makes sense to you -- not necessarily visible to others, but you know where everything is
answers.B: Clean and presentable. You feel off if it isn't.
answers.C: Functional but a little chaotic. Things tend to pile up unless you do a deliberate reset.
answers.D: Varies a lot. It reflects exactly where your head is at.

new_questions.text: Someone you've just met is clearly struggling with something. You don't know them well. What do you do?

answers.A: Hang back and observe. Once you understand the situation better, you'll know if you can actually help.
answers.B: Step in and offer something concrete. You don't need a relationship established first.
answers.C: Make conversation -- not about the struggle, just to normalize things and ease the tension
answers.D: Ask directly if they want help. You'd rather be told no than assume they don't need anything.

new_questions.text: You're performing well -- better than expected, actually. How do you sit with that?

answers.A: Good, but it just raises the bar for what you expect from yourself next time
answers.B: It's validating. You want the people who matter to you to notice it too.
answers.C: You're pleased, but you're already thinking about where the gaps are. Something can always be tighter.
answers.D: Honestly surprised every time, even when you probably shouldn't be

new_questions.text: You're working on something and hit a problem you can't immediately solve. What's your first move?

answers.A: Break it down. Figure out which part is actually the problem before trying anything.
answers.B: Start trying things. Iteration often gets you somewhere faster than analysis.
answers.C: Ask someone who probably knows. No point burning time on something someone else has already solved.
answers.D: Step away for a bit. Your brain usually catches up once you stop forcing it.

new_questions.text: Think about the last time you felt genuinely at ease -- not just not-stressed, but actually comfortable. What was happening?

answers.A: You were doing something useful. Productivity feels like rest for you.
answers.B: You were with people you like, and nothing in particular needed to happen.
answers.C: You were alone, doing something that belongs entirely to you.
answers.D: You were in motion -- working toward something, seeing it take shape.

new_questions.text: What tends to be true of how you talk to people you trust versus people you don't know well?

answers.A: Not much difference, honestly. You're pretty consistent either way.
answers.B: Big difference. The version people see first is a lot more polished than who you actually are.
answers.C: You become warmer, but not fundamentally different -- the core is the same, you just relax more
answers.D: You open up about specific things -- thoughts, problems, opinions -- but you don't really change registers

new_questions.text: Someone asks you to do something you're good at, but not the way you'd do it.

answers.A: You do it their way. Results matter more than method.
answers.B: You do it their way, but you note where it cost efficiency.
answers.C: You do it your way and explain afterward why that was better.
answers.D: You ask why they want it done that way before starting.

new_questions.text: You've been around someone long enough to form an opinion of them. How do they find out?

answers.A: They probably don't, unless they ask directly.
answers.B: It comes out in how you treat them, not in anything you say.
answers.C: You tell them, because pretending you haven't formed one is a waste of both your time.
answers.D: You'll drop a comment here and there. Nothing heavy.

new_questions.text: You're good at something. A teammate is struggling with the same thing. You...

answers.A: Step in before they have to ask.
answers.B: Wait until they ask, then help without making it a lesson.
answers.C: Point them toward resources. You don't want to create dependency.
answers.D: Help them and use the chance to build a real working relationship.

new_questions.text: You've had a long day and someone wants to have a conversation. Your honest response is...

answers.A: Fine, but keep it short.
answers.B: Sure. Talking is how you reset anyway.
answers.C: Not right now. You need quiet first.
answers.D: Depends on who it is.

new_questions.text: When you hit your limit with someone, what gives it away?

answers.A: You get quieter. Shorter answers, less eye contact.
answers.B: You say so directly. Letting it build doesn't help anyone.
answers.C: You pull back from the situation entirely until you can handle it.
answers.D: You stay engaged but stop going out of your way for them.

new_questions.text: Your work just got criticized in front of others. You...

answers.A: Listen, evaluate whether they're right, and respond accordingly.
answers.B: Feel it, but keep that internal. You address the criticism, not the feeling.
answers.C: Address it in the moment so the record is clear.
answers.D: Let it go for now. You'll revisit it privately when the stakes are lower.

new_questions.text: What's your version of unwinding?

answers.A: Doing something physical or hands-on, solo.
answers.B: Something low-key with one or two people you trust.
answers.C: Something you can fully disappear into for an hour.
answers.D: A bit of everything. You're not that consistent about it.

new_questions.text: You're proud of something you did, but no one noticed. You feel...

answers.A: Fine. You know what you accomplished.
answers.B: A little deflated, if you're honest, but you move on.
answers.C: Like you might mention it once, casually, and see if it lands.
answers.D: Like you should have made it more visible. Lesson for next time.

new_questions.text: There's one thing you won't compromise on, even for someone you care about. When that line comes up, you...

answers.A: Hold it without making it into a confrontation.
answers.B: Say it plainly and early so there's no confusion later.
answers.C: Find out if they actually need you to cross it, or just assume they do.
answers.D: Hold it, but feel the weight of it. This is the one that costs you.

new_questions.text: You've been sitting with something heavy for a few days. You haven't told anyone. What's closest to what's happening?

answers.A: You're waiting until you understand it well enough to explain it clearly
answers.B: You'd rather just outlast it than make it anyone else's problem
answers.C: You've thought about saying something but can't figure out how to start
answers.D: You're not sure it's worth bringing up -- it might just resolve itself

new_questions.text: Someone gives you a genuine compliment on something you worked hard on. What do you do with it?

answers.A: Thank them, then quietly wonder if they actually mean it
answers.B: Redirect -- mention the team, or what could have been better
answers.C: Accept it, but it sits in you longer than it probably should
answers.D: Appreciate it in the moment, move on, don't think about it much

new_questions.text: You've been carrying something quietly -- a task, a worry, a responsibility. Someone notices and offers to help. You:

answers.A: Decline. They shouldn't have to deal with it, and you'd rather handle it yourself.
answers.B: Let them help but feel faintly guilty about it the whole time
answers.C: Say you're fine, then come back to them later when you've thought about whether you actually need help
answers.D: Accept, but keep a close eye on how they're handling it -- easier if you're still involved

new_questions.text: You want to check in on someone you care about. What's most likely to happen?

answers.A: You find a practical reason to reach out -- a question, a task, something useful
answers.B: You watch how they're doing from a distance and wait for the right moment
answers.C: You write something, then sit on it for a while before deciding whether to send it
answers.D: You show up near them and let the conversation happen on its own, or not

new_questions.text: When you imagine the version of yourself you most want to be, what does it look like?

answers.A: Someone who's genuinely useful -- whose presence makes things easier for the people around them
answers.B: Someone who understands things clearly and can find peace in that understanding
answers.C: Someone who doesn't carry every mistake forward, who can set it down and move on
answers.D: Someone who can connect with people without it taking so much effort

new_questions.text: Something reminded you of a mistake you made months ago. No one else brought it up. What happens?

answers.A: You replay it briefly, find something to do, let it pass
answers.B: You try to figure out what you'd do differently -- it becomes useful, eventually
answers.C: It stays with you for longer than makes sense, even though you know that
answers.D: You put it away -- it happened, it's done, no point in running it back

new_questions.text: A quieter night, nothing urgent. What does that time actually look like for you?

answers.A: Some kind of project or task that you set your own pace on -- work that feels like rest
answers.B: Low stimulation, low noise -- something easy or nothing at all
answers.C: Somewhere between rest and thinking -- your mind goes where it wants
answers.D: Tending to something, or someone -- that's when you do the small things that matter to you

new_questions.text: When someone tries to get to know you better -- asks about you directly, shows they're curious -- your first instinct is:

answers.A: Answer carefully, watch how they respond, go from there
answers.B: Give them something true but not the whole thing -- enough to feel honest
answers.C: Turn it back around on them; you're more comfortable asking than being asked
answers.D: Help them with something instead -- that's easier than explaining yourself

new_questions.text: You've been carrying a mistake for a while. What does it actually feel like?

answers.A: A standard to close the gap on
answers.B: Proof you're not as reliable as you thought
answers.C: Something you keep returning to, even when you try not to
answers.D: A variable that still needs explaining

new_questions.text: Someone you respect sits down and tells you something they've never told anyone. What's your first instinct?

answers.A: To do something useful with what they told you
answers.B: To protect them from whatever caused it
answers.C: To be honest about something you've been holding back too
answers.D: To stay very still and let them finish

new_questions.text: You do something genuinely well, and someone notices. What happens next?

answers.A: You wonder if they'd still think so if they saw the full picture
answers.B: It gives you permission to feel good for about thirty seconds, then you move on
answers.C: You start thinking about whether you can do it again
answers.D: You thank them and immediately find something to do

new_questions.text: You're with someone you care about and you realize you've said more than you meant to. What do you do?

answers.A: Change the subject as fast as possible
answers.B: Own it. You said it, so it's out now.
answers.C: Stay quiet and hope they don't press on it
answers.D: Make a small joke to soften the landing

new_questions.text: What's the thing that most reliably makes you feel like yourself?

answers.A: A task you're good at, done alone
answers.B: Someone you trust being nearby
answers.C: Something you make or maintain with real care
answers.D: Knowing exactly where you stand

new_questions.text: When you're around someone new, what are you actually paying attention to?

answers.A: Whether they're going to need something from you
answers.B: Whether they can be trusted with what you already know
answers.C: What they're like when no one's watching
answers.D: Whether you're being read correctly

new_questions.text: Someone gives you real responsibility over something that matters to them. Your first thought?

answers.A: You need to understand it completely before you touch it
answers.B: You don't want to be the reason it goes wrong
answers.C: Finally -- something you can actually do
answers.D: You're honored, and slightly terrified of that feeling

new_questions.text: You've been useful to someone in a real way. They try to thank you. What do you feel?

answers.A: Glad, but also already thinking about the next thing
answers.B: Faintly uncomfortable, like you owe them a better version of yourself
answers.C: Like maybe you've finally earned a small bit of ground
answers.D: You want to tell them it was nothing, and you mostly mean it

new_questions.text: What does your loyalty look like in practice?

answers.A: Showing up prepared, every time, without being asked
answers.B: Staying close enough to catch them before they fall
answers.C: Making sure the work reflects well on both of you
answers.D: Keeping what they've told you to yourself

new_questions.text: Someone compliments your work -- sincerely, not just to be polite. What happens?

answers.A: You say thank you and move on. The job was the point, not the recognition.
answers.B: You appreciate it, but you're already thinking about what you should have done better.
answers.C: It lands, more than you'd like to admit. You replay it a little.
answers.D: It makes you slightly uncomfortable. You'd rather they just said nothing.

new_questions.text: You've been working alongside someone for a long time. How do you show you trust them?

answers.A: You stop double-checking their work. That's the whole signal.
answers.B: You tell them things you wouldn't normally say out loud.
answers.C: You give them tasks you'd normally handle yourself.
answers.D: You probably don't show it explicitly. They'd have to read it from context.

new_questions.text: When you make a mistake that affects someone else, the worst part is...

answers.A: That you were the one who failed -- the standard you hold yourself to
answers.B: That it caused real harm. The internal part doesn't matter compared to that.
answers.C: Facing them afterward. Even if they're fine with it, you aren't.
answers.D: Not knowing if they'll look at you differently going forward.

new_questions.text: You're asked to do something you've already done a hundred times. How do you approach it?

answers.A: Same as always. The routine is part of what makes you reliable.
answers.B: You look for what can be done better this time.
answers.C: You do it well, but it costs more energy than it should.
answers.D: You find something worth focusing on, even in repetition. There's always something.

new_questions.text: Something goes wrong and it's not your fault -- but you were there. How do you handle it?

answers.A: You focus on the fix, not the fault. Questions of blame come later.
answers.B: You go quiet and work through what you could have seen coming.
answers.C: You take on whatever piece of the recovery you can carry.
answers.D: You keep your composure outwardly. Internally, it sits with you for a while.

new_questions.text: You have a side of yourself that almost no one sees. What's closest to what that looks like?

answers.A: Softer. More affected by things than you let on.
answers.B: Dryer. You're funnier to yourself than you are to anyone else.
answers.C: More uncertain. The competence people see is something you have to keep constructing.
answers.D: More attached. To certain people, certain things -- more than you'd say directly.

new_questions.text: What's the clearest sign that you're actually comfortable with someone?

answers.A: You stop managing how you come across around them.
answers.B: You let them help you with something. That doesn't happen often.
answers.C: You give them your honest opinion when you'd usually stay quiet.
answers.D: You stay near them without needing a reason for it.

new_questions.text: The thing you want most from the people close to you is...

answers.A: Steadiness. Be the same person every time.
answers.B: To be seen accurately -- not idealized, not underestimated.
answers.C: To be given space, and to know that space isn't a problem.
answers.D: To know they'd stay even when you're not performing at your best.
